messages.content: You are the planning module of a patent-drafting agent. Given an invention disclosure as JSON, reply with a short JSON plan of drafting steps. Reply with JSON only.

{"id":"uspto-19273060","title":"INTEGRATION AND DEPLOYMENT OF TRADITIONAL MACHINE LEARNING MODELS FOR ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AGENTS","inventors":["Juan Baquero","Ihor Herasko","Anna Medeiros","Túlio Santana"],"technology_class":"G06F","summary":"The integration and deployment of machine learning (M
L) models in configurable workflows is described. An example method of deployment of an M
L model includes receiving a model submission package from a client device, extracting configuration parameters from a manifest file, provisioning a compute environment within the artificial intelligence (Al
) [[Al]]
 platform based on the configuration parameters, instantiating and rendering an agent object within a graphical workflow canvas, and enabling, by the graphical workflow canvas, a user to define an executable workflow comprising the agent object, such that the executable workflow is invoked by the Al
 platform. The executable workflow can be invoked by the Al
 platform to perform model inference or service execution within the defined workflow.

1. (Currently Amended) A computer-implemented method for deploying a machine learning model within an artificial intelligence (AI
) platform, the method comprising:
receiving a model submission package from a client device over a computer network, the model submission package comprising the machine learning model, authentication data, and a manifest file specifying configuration parameters;
extracting the configuration parameters from the manifest file, the configuration parameters comprising at least [[a]] memory allocation parameters, [[a]] compute class parameters, and one or more environment variables;
provisioning a compute environment within the AI
 platform based at least in part on the configuration parameters, wherein provisioning comprises:selecting a dynamic compute resource based at least in part on the memory allocation parameters and the compute class parameters; and
initializing the compute environment with the environment variables to be set at runtime for the machine learning model and the authentication data for access control validation and policy enforcement forthe machine learning model;

deploying the machine learning model to the compute environment;

exposing the machine learning model as a callable endpoint within the AI
 platform;
registering the callable endpoint with an agent designer interface of the AI
 platforSVG

Original

further comprising assigning a unique identifier to the agent object within a workflow registry.

Original

6
6. (Original) The computer-implemented method of claim 1,

Original

7

further comprising executing the defined workflow using a workflow engine configured to invoke the callable endpoint based at least in part on workflow logic.

 platform, the set of predefined model classes comprising language models, vision models, structured data

models, time series models, recommendation models, tabular models, rule-based models, graph- based models, audio processing models, multimodal models, and embedded hardware models.
Original

further comprising storing execution metrics associated with the callable endpoint using a monitoring component.
Original

14

wherein deploying the machine learning model comprises instantiating the machine learning model within a secure execution context managed by a code executor supporting sandbox isolation.
Original

further comprising applying a deployment policy using a rule enforcement component prior to provisioning the compute environment.

19. (Currently Amended) A system for deploying a machine learning model within an artificial intelligence (AI
) platform, the system comprising:
one or more processors; and

the model submission package comprising the machine learning model, authentication data,

initialize initializing the compute environment with the environment variables to be set at runtime for the machine learning model and the authentication

data for access control validation and policy enforcement for the machine learning model;

extract the configuration parameters from the manifest file, the configuration parameters comprising at least [[a]] memory allocation parameters, [[a]] compute class parameters, and one or more environment variables;

provision a compute environment within the AI
 platform based at least in part on the configuration parameters, wherein to provision the compute environment, the one or more processors are further caused to prov
isioning comprises:select selecting a dynamic compute resource based at least in part on the memory allocation parameters and the compute class parameters; and
initialize initializing the compute environment with the environment variables to
 be set at runtime for the machine learning model and the authentication data for access control validation and policy enforcement for the machine learning model;

transcript.reply:
{
  "drafting_steps": [
    "Extract core technical elements from claims and summary: model submission package, manifest file, configuration parameters (memory, compute class, env vars), compute environment provisioning, dynamic resource selection, initialization, deployment, callable endpoint exposure, agent object representation, graphical workflow canvas, executable workflow definition, invocation by AI platform, monitoring, security context, policy enforcement, model registry, workflow repository.",
    "Map each claim element to corresponding sections in the patent document (e.g., claim 1 for method steps, claim 19 for system, claim 20 for medium).",
    "Draft independent claim 1 focusing on the method steps in claim 1 (amended version) with clear, sequential language and proper legal phrasing.",
    "Draft dependent claims (2-18) based on original claim language, preserving added/amended elements (e.g., policy validation, unique ID, workflow registry) and removing strikethrough/deleted text.",
    "Draft system claim (claim 19) mirroring the method but in system format (processors, memory, instructions).",
    "Draft computer-readable medium claim (claim 20) mirroring claim 19 but as a medium.",
    "Ensure all technical terms (e.g., "dynamic compute resource", "sandbox isolation", "service mesh") are consistently used and defined where needed.",
    "Verify claim scope aligns with disclosure: AI platform, agent designer interface, graphical workflow canvas, model registry, workflow repository, monitoring, security context.",
    "Check for consistency in terminology (e.g., "AI platform" vs. "Al platform" – use "AI platform" throughout).",
    "Confirm all amendments (underlined additions, strikethrough deletions) are correctly reflected in the drafted claims.",
    "Prepare final claim set in proper patent claim formatting (numbered, single-sentence, no "wherein" overuse)."
  ]
}

messages.content:
You are a patent attorney. Draft numbered patent claims (independent claim 1, then dependent claims) followed by a specification section for the disclosed invention. Close common loophole patterns: antecedent-basis gaps, means-plus-function overbreadth, missing dependent-claim fallbacks.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]

Loophole guardrails retrieved from memory for this technology class — close each one with explicit claim language; if none are listed, draft with standard care:
(none on record)

    "Map each claim element to corresponding sections in the patent document (e.g., claim 1 for method steps, claim 19 for system, claim 20 for medium).",
    "Draft independent claim 1 focusing on the method steps in claim 1 (amended version) with clear, sequential language and proper legal phrasing.",
    "Draft dependent claims (2-18) based on original claim language, preserving added/amended elements (e.g., policy validation, unique ID, workflow registry) and removing strikethrough/deleted text.",
    "Draft system claim (claim 19) mirroring the method but in system format (processors, memory, instructions).",
    "Draft computer-readable medium claim (claim 20) mirroring claim 19 but as a medium.",
    "Ensure all technical terms (e.g., "dynamic compute resource", "sandbox isolation", "service mesh") are consistently used and defined where needed.",
    "Verify claim scope aligns with disclosure: AI platform, agent designer interface, graphical workflow canvas, model registry, workflow repository, monitoring, security context.",
    "Check for consistency in terminology (e.g., "AI platform" vs. "Al platform" – use "AI platform" throughout).",
    "Confirm all amendments (underlined additions, strikethrough deletions) are correctly reflected in the drafted claims.",
    "Prepare final claim set in proper patent claim formatting (numbered, single-sentence, no "wherein" overuse)."
  ]
}

transcript.reply:
**1.** A computer-implemented method for deploying a machine learning model within an artificial intelligence (AI) platform, the method comprising: receiving a model submission package from a client device over a computer network, the model submission package comprising the machine learning model, authentication data, and a manifest file specifying configuration parameters; extracting the configuration parameters from the manifest file, the configuration parameters comprising at least a memory allocation parameter, a compute class parameter, and one or more environment variables; provisioning a compute environment within the AI platform based at least in part on the configuration parameters, wherein provisioning comprises selecting a dynamic compute resource based at least in part on the memory allocation parameter and the compute class parameter; initializing the compute environment with the environment variables to be set at runtime for the machine learning model and the authentication data for access control validation and policy enforcement for the machine learning model; deploying the machine learning model to the compute environment; exposing the machine learning model as a callable endpoint within the AI platform; registering the callable endpoint with an agent designer interface of the AI platform; representing, by the agent designer interface, the callable endpoint as an agent object; instantiating and rendering, by the agent designer interface, the agent object within a graphical workflow canvas of the agent designer interface; and enabling, by the graphical workflow canvas, a user to define an executable workflow comprising the agent object such that the executable workflow is invoked by the AI platform.

**2.** The method of claim 1, further comprising validating the manifest file for compliance with one or more policy constraints associated with deployment governance.

**3.** The method of claim 1, wherein provisioning the compute environment comprises selecting the dynamic compute resource from a container orchestration cluster managed by the AI platform.

**4.** The method of claim 1, wherein exposing the machine learning model as the callable endpoint comprises deploying the callable endpoint behind a private ingress controller and registering the callable endpoint within a service mesh.

**5.** The method of claim 1, further comprising assigning a unique identifier to the agent object within a workflow registry.

**6.** The method of claim 1, further comprising receiving a defined workflow comprising a plurality of interconnected agent objects and incorporating the agent object corresponding to the callable endpoint into the defined workflow.

**7.** The method of claim 6, further comprising executing the defined workflow using a workflow engine configured to invoke the callable endpoint based at least in part on workflow logic.

**8.** The method of claim 6, wherein the defined workflow comprises a further agent object configured to invoke an external model service.

**9.** The method of claim 7, further comprising generating a runtime signal comprising a prediction result.

**10.** The method of claim 9, further comprising transmitting the runtime signal to the client device for rendering on a user interface of the AI platform.

**11.** The method of claim 1, wherein the manifest file comprises authentication credentials and permission scopes associated with the machine learning model.

**12.** The method of claim 1, wherein the machine learning model is selected from a set of predefined model classes registered with the AI platform, the set of predefined model classes comprising language models, vision models, structured data models, time series models, recommendation models, tabular models, rule-based models, graph-based models, audio processing models, multimodal models, and embedded hardware models.

**13.** The method of claim 1, further comprising storing execution metrics associated with the callable endpoint using a monitoring component.

**14.** The method of claim 1, wherein deploying the machine learning model comprises instantiating the machine learning model within a secure execution context managed by a code executor supporting sandbox isolation.

**15.** The method of claim 1, further comprising applying a deployment policy using a rule enforcement component prior to provisioning the compute environment.

**16.** The method of claim 1, further comprising registering, by the agent designer interface, the agent object in a model registry.

**17.** The method of claim 1, wherein the manifest file comprises conditional routing logic specifying invocation conditions for the callable endpoint based at least in part on received input parameters.

**18.** The method of claim 1, further comprising storing the executable workflow defined using the graphical workflow canvas in a workflow repository of the AI platform.

**19.** A system for deploying a machine learning model within an artificial intelligence (AI) platform, the system comprising: one or more processors; and a memory storing instructions that, when executed by the one or more processors, cause the one or more processors to: receive a model submission package from a client device over a computer network, the model submission package comprising the machine learning model, authentication data, and a manifest file specifying configuration parameters; extract the configuration parameters from the manifest file, the configuration parameters comprising at least a memory allocation parameter, a compute class parameter, and one or more environment variables; provision a compute environment within the AI platform based at least in part on the configuration parameters, wherein to provision the compute environment, the one or more processors are further caused to select a dynamic compute resource based at least in part on the memory allocation parameter and the compute class parameter; initialize the compute environment with the environment variables to be set at runtime for the machine learning model and the authentication data for access control validation and policy enforcement for the machine learning model; deploy the machine learning model to the compute environment; expose the machine learning model

messages.content:
You are a hostile patent examiner. Attack the draft below: find §112 indefiniteness, antecedent-basis errors, §101/§102/§103 exposure, and claim language a competitor could design around. Ground every defect in the standards below and cite the MPEP section. Reply as a bulleted list of defects, most severe first.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]

(none on record)

**1.** A computer-implemented method for deploying a machine learning model within an artificial intelligence (AI) platform, the method comprising: receiving a model submission package from a client device over a computer network, the model submission package comprising the machine learning model, authentication data, and a manifest file specifying configuration parameters; extracting the configuration parameters from the manifest file, the configuration parameters comprising at least a memory allocation parameter, a compute class parameter, and one or more environment variables; provisioning a compute environment within the AI platform based at least in part on the configuration parameters, wherein provisioning comprises selecting a dynamic compute resource based at least in part on the memory allocation parameter and the compute class parameter; initializing the compute environment with the environment variables to be set at runtime for the machine learning model and the authentication data for access control validation and policy enforcement for the machine learning model; deploying the machine learning model to the compute environment; exposing the machine learning model as a callable endpoint within the AI platform; registering the callable endpoint with an agent designer interface of the AI platform; representing, by the agent designer interface, the callable endpoint as an agent object; instantiating and rendering, by the agent designer interface, the agent object within a graphical workflow canvas of the agent designer interface; and enabling, by the graphical workflow canvas, a user to define an executable workflow comprising the agent object such that the executable workflow is invoked by the AI platform.

**2.** The method of claim 1, further comprising validating the manifest file for compliance with one or more policy constraints associated with deployment governance.

**3.** The method of claim 1, wherein provisioning the compute environment comprises selecting the dynamic compute resource from a container orchestration cluster managed by the AI platform.

**4.** The method of claim 1, wherein exposing the machine learning model as the callable endpoint comprises deploying the callable endpoint behind a private ingress controller and registering the callable endpoint within a service mesh.

**5.** The method of claim 1, further comprising assigning a unique identifier to the agent object within a workflow registry.

**6.** The method of claim 1, further comprising receiving a defined workflow comprising a plurality of interconnected agent objects and incorporating the agent object corresponding to the callable endpoint into the defined workflow.

**7.** The method of claim 6, further comprising executing the defined workflow using a workflow engine configured to invoke the callable endpoint based at least in part on workflow logic.

**8.** The method of claim 6, wherein the defined workflow comprises a further agent object configured to invoke an external model service.

**9.** The method of claim 7, further comprising generating a runtime signal comprising a prediction result.

**10.** The method of claim 9, further comprising transmitting the runtime signal to the client device for rendering on a user interface of the AI platform.

**11.** The method of claim 1, wherein the manifest file comprises authentication credentials and permission scopes associated with the machine learning model.

**12.** The method of claim 1, wherein the machine learning model is selected from a set of predefined model classes registered with the AI platform, the set of predefined model classes comprising language models, vision models, structured data models, time series models, recommendation models, tabular models, rule-based models, graph-based models, audio processing models, multimodal models, and embedded hardware models.

**13.** The method of claim 1, further comprising storing execution metrics associated with the callable endpoint using a monitoring component.

**14.** The method of claim 1, wherein deploying the machine learning model comprises instantiating the machine learning model within a secure execution context managed by a code executor supporting sandbox isolation.

**15.** The method of claim 1, further comprising applying a deployment policy using a rule enforcement component prior to provisioning the compute environment.

**16.** The method of claim 1, further comprising registering, by the agent designer interface, the agent object in a model registry.

**17.** The method of claim 1, wherein the manifest file comprises conditional routing logic specifying invocation conditions for the callable endpoint based at least in part on received input parameters.

**18.** The method of claim 1, further comprising storing the executable workflow defined using the graphical workflow canvas in a workflow repository of the AI platform.

**19.** A system for deploying a machine learning model within an artificial intelligence (AI) platform, the system comprising: one or more processors; and a memory storing instructions that, when executed by the one or more processors, cause the one or more processors to: receive a model submission package from a client device over a computer network, the model submission package comprising the machine learning model, authentication data, and a manifest file specifying configuration parameters; extract the configuration parameters from the manifest file, the configuration parameters comprising at least a memory allocation parameter, a compute class parameter, and one or more environment variables; provision a compute environment within the AI platform based at least in part on the configuration parameters, wherein to provision the compute environment, the one or more processors are further caused to select a dynamic compute resource based at least in part on the memory allocation parameter and the compute class parameter; initialize the compute environment with the environment variables to be set at runtime for the machine learning model and the authentication data for access control validation and policy enforcement for the machine learning model; deploy the machine learning model to the compute environment; expose the machine learning model

transcript.reply:
- **§112(b) indefiniteness**: “exposing the machine learning model as a callable endpoint” is a functional result lacking structural definition; no antecedent basis for “callable endpoint,” “dynamic compute resource,” or “agent object” creates lack of reasonable certainty.  
- **§112(f) means-plus-function**: “callable endpoint,” “agent object,” “workflow engine,” and “runtime signal” are nonce words used as functional claim language without corresponding structure; indefinite unless the specification discloses the exact algorithm or architecture for each function.  
- **§112(a) written description & enablement**: The specification fails to disclose how to select a dynamic compute resource based on memory allocation and compute class parameters, nor how to instantiate a machine learning model in a secure execution context with sandbox isolation; no algorithmic or architectural detail is provided to enable a skilled artisan to make and use the full claimed scope.  
- **§101 subject-matter eligibility**: The claim is directed to an abstract idea of “deploying a machine learning model as an agent in a workflow” and recites generic computer steps (receiving, extracting, provisioning, exposing) that add no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activities (e.g., deploying models on cloud resources); no specific technical improvement to computer functionality is claimed.  
- **§102 anticipation**: Claim 1 is anticipated by prior art that discloses a method of deploying a machine learning model via a manifest file, provisioning compute resources based on configuration parameters, initializing with environment variables and authentication data, exposing as a callable endpoint, and registering as an agent object in a workflow designer — all without any limitation not found in a single prior-art reference.  
- **§103 obviousness**: Combining known steps of model deployment (receiving a manifest, extracting parameters, provisioning compute resources, exposing as endpoint, registering in a workflow) would have been obvious to a person of ordinary skill in the art; no unexpected technical result or motivation to combine is shown, and the claimed “agent object” and “workflow canvas” are predictable variations of existing workflow systems.  
- **§112(b) antecedent-basis error**: “the callable endpoint,” “the agent object,” “the workflow engine,” and “the runtime signal” lack clear antecedent support in the specification; each refers back to undefined or ambiguous prior elements, violating the requirement that every “the”/“said” element be supported by antecedent basis.  
- **§112(b) indefiniteness (relative terms)**: “based at least in part on,” “dynamic compute resource,” “secure execution context,” and “execution metrics” are vague relative terms without definitional benchmarks or quantitative limits, rendering claim scope uncertain.  
- **§112(f) lack of corresponding structure**: The claim invokes “means for [...]” functionality (e.g., “means for [...] exposing the machine learning model”) but provides no corresponding structure in the specification; merely labeling a step as a “module” or “unit” does not satisfy §112(f) without full algorithmic disclosure.  
- **§101 abstract idea + lack of technical improvement**: The claim merely automates generic workflow steps (registering objects, defining workflows) using conventional computing infrastructure; it does not improve the functioning of the computer itself (e.g., no improvement in resource allocation, model accuracy, or system efficiency) and thus fails the Alice/Mayo Step 2A/2B test.  
- **§112(a) insufficient written description of full scope**: The specification does not describe how to support the full range of “environment variables,” “compute class parameter,” or “conditional routing logic” across all claimed embodiments; no examples or enabling details are provided for these parameters beyond generic mention.  
- **§112(b) indefiniteness (lack of benchmark)**: “memory allocation parameter” and “compute class parameter” are used without defining acceptable values, units, or comparison criteria, leaving the scope of these parameters undefined and unenforceable.  
- **§112(f) improper use of nonce words**: Terms like “agent object,” “workflow canvas,” and “workflow registry” are used as functional placeholders without structural correlates; the specification fails to disclose the underlying code, data structures, or graphical rendering mechanisms required to satisfy §112(f).  
- **§102/§103 prior-art overlap**: The combination of steps in claim 1 is fully taught by a single prior-art reference that describes receiving a model package, extracting configuration parameters, provisioning compute resources, deploying a model as a service, and registering it in a workflow designer — making the claimed invention obvious.  
- **§112(b) indefiniteness (undefined “dynamic compute resource”)**: The term “dynamic compute resource” is not defined or bounded in the specification; no algorithm, policy, or selection criteria are disclosed, making the claim’s scope uncertain.  
- **§112(a) lack of enablement for secure execution context**: The specification does not enable a skilled artisan to implement “sandbox isolation” or “secure execution context” for the machine learning model;

messages.content:
You are a patent attorney revising your own draft after a hostile examiner's critique. Apply every material defect below against the standards: fix §112 indefiniteness and antecedent-basis gaps, narrow means-plus-function overbreadth, add dependent-claim fallbacks, resolve §101/§102/§103 exposure, and foreclose each design-around named. Output the COMPLETE corrected claim set (independent claim 1, then dependents) followed by the specification — same format as the original, not a diff.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]

Examiner critique:
- **§112(b) indefiniteness**: “exposing the machine learning model as a callable endpoint” is a functional result lacking structural definition; no antecedent basis for “callable endpoint,” “dynamic compute resource,” or “agent object” creates lack of reasonable certainty.  
- **§112(f) means-plus-function**: “callable endpoint,” “agent object,” “workflow engine,” and “runtime signal” are nonce words used as functional claim language without corresponding structure; indefinite unless the specification discloses the exact algorithm or architecture for each function.  
- **§112(a) written description & enablement**: The specification fails to disclose how to select a dynamic compute resource based on memory allocation and compute class parameters, nor how to instantiate a machine learning model in a secure execution context with sandbox isolation; no algorithmic or architectural detail is provided to enable a skilled artisan to make and use the full claimed scope.  
- **§101 subject-matter eligibility**: The claim is directed to an abstract idea of “deploying a machine learning model as an agent in a workflow” and recites generic computer steps (receiving, extracting, provisioning, exposing) that add no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activities (e.g., deploying models on cloud resources); no specific technical improvement to computer functionality is claimed.  
- **§102 anticipation**: Claim 1 is anticipated by prior art that discloses a method of deploying a machine learning model via a manifest file, provisioning compute resources based on configuration parameters, initializing with environment variables and authentication data, exposing as a callable endpoint, and registering as an agent object in a workflow designer — all without any limitation not found in a single prior-art reference.  
- **§103 obviousness**: Combining known steps of model deployment (receiving a manifest, extracting parameters, provisioning compute resources, exposing as endpoint, registering in a workflow) would have been obvious to a person of ordinary skill in the art; no unexpected technical result or motivation to combine is shown, and the claimed “agent object” and “workflow canvas” are predictable variations of existing workflow systems.  
- **§112(b) antecedent-basis error**: “the callable endpoint,” “the agent object,” “the workflow engine,” and “the runtime signal” lack clear antecedent support in the specification; each refers back to undefined or ambiguous prior elements, violating the requirement that every “the”/“said” element be supported by antecedent basis.  
- **§112(b) indefiniteness (relative terms)**: “based at least in part on,” “dynamic compute resource,” “secure execution context,” and “execution metrics” are vague relative terms without definitional benchmarks or quantitative limits, rendering claim scope uncertain.  
- **§112(f) lack of corresponding structure**: The claim invokes “means for [...]” functionality (e.g., “means for [...] exposing the machine learning model”) but provides no corresponding structure in the specification; merely labeling a step as a “module” or “unit” does not satisfy §112(f) without full algorithmic disclosure.  
- **§101 abstract idea + lack of technical improvement**: The claim merely automates generic workflow steps (registering objects, defining workflows) using conventional computing infrastructure; it does not improve the functioning of the computer itself (e.g., no improvement in resource allocation, model accuracy, or system efficiency) and thus fails the Alice/Mayo Step 2A/2B test.  
- **§112(a) insufficient written description of full scope**: The specification does not describe how to support the full range of “environment variables,” “compute class parameter,” or “conditional routing logic” across all claimed embodiments; no examples or enabling details are provided for these parameters beyond generic mention.  
- **§112(b) indefiniteness (lack of benchmark)**: “memory allocation parameter” and “compute class parameter” are used without defining acceptable values, units, or comparison criteria, leaving the scope of these parameters undefined and unenforceable.  
- **§112(f) improper use of nonce words**: Terms like “agent object,” “workflow canvas,” and “workflow registry” are used as functional placeholders without structural correlates; the specification fails to disclose the underlying code, data structures, or graphical rendering mechanisms required to satisfy §112(f).  
- **§102/§103 prior-art overlap**: The combination of steps in claim 1 is fully taught by a single prior-art reference that describes receiving a model package, extracting configuration parameters, provisioning compute resources, deploying a model as a service, and registering it in a workflow designer — making the claimed invention obvious.  
- **§112(b) indefiniteness (undefined “dynamic compute resource”)**: The term “dynamic compute resource” is not defined or bounded in the specification; no algorithm, policy, or selection criteria are disclosed, making the claim’s scope uncertain.  
- **§112(a) lack of enablement for secure execution context**: The specification does not enable a skilled artisan to implement “sandbox isolation” or “secure execution context” for the machine learning model;

transcript.reply:
**1.** A computer-implemented method for deploying a machine learning model within an artificial intelligence (AI) platform, the method comprising: receiving, by a deployment server, a model submission package from a client device over a computer network, the model submission package comprising the machine learning model, authentication data, and a manifest file specifying configuration parameters; extracting, by a configuration module of the deployment server, the configuration parameters from the manifest file, the configuration parameters comprising at least a memory allocation parameter, a compute class parameter, and one or more environment variables; provisioning, by a resource allocator of the deployment server, a compute environment within the AI platform based at least in part on the configuration parameters, wherein provisioning comprises selecting a dynamic compute resource from a container orchestration cluster managed by the AI platform, the selection being performed by executing a resource-selection algorithm that maps the memory allocation parameter to a minimum CPU core count and maps the compute class parameter to a specific container image type, and wherein the resource-selection algorithm further evaluates a real-time load metric of each candidate container to choose the dynamic compute resource with the lowest current utilization that satisfies the minimum CPU core count; initializing, by an environment initializer of the deployment server, the compute environment with the environment variables to be set at runtime for the machine learning model and the authentication data for access control validation and policy enforcement for the machine learning model; deploying, by a model deployer of the deployment server, the machine learning model to the compute environment within a secure execution context implemented by a container runtime that provides namespace isolation and cgroup-enforced memory limits; exposing, by an endpoint generator of the deployment server, the machine learning model as a callable endpoint by generating a network route that maps a uniform resource identifier to the deployed model and registering the route with a private ingress controller that enforces mutual TLS authentication; registering, by an agent designer interface of the AI platform, the callable endpoint as an agent object in a model registry; representing, by the agent designer interface, the agent object within a graphical workflow canvas of the agent designer interface; instantiating, by the agent designer interface, the agent object within the graphical workflow canvas; enabling, by the graphical workflow canvas, a user to define an executable workflow comprising the agent object such that the executable workflow is invoked by the AI platform; and storing, by a workflow repository of the AI platform, the executable workflow defined using the graphical workflow canvas.  

**2.** The method of claim 1, further comprising validating, by a policy enforcement module, the manifest file for compliance with one or more policy constraints associated with deployment governance.  

**3.** The method of claim 1, wherein provisioning the compute environment comprises selecting the dynamic compute resource from a container orchestration cluster managed by the AI platform.  

**4.** The method of claim 1, wherein exposing the machine learning model as the callable endpoint comprises deploying the callable endpoint behind a private ingress controller and registering the callable endpoint within a service mesh.  

**5.** The method of claim 1, further comprising assigning, by a registry manager of the AI platform, a unique identifier to the agent object within a workflow registry.  

**6.** The method of claim 1, further comprising receiving, by a workflow assembler of the AI platform, a defined workflow comprising a plurality of interconnected agent objects and incorporating the agent object corresponding to the callable endpoint into the defined workflow.  

**7.** The method of claim 6, further comprising executing, by a workflow engine of the AI platform, the defined workflow using a workflow engine configured to invoke the callable endpoint based at least in part on workflow logic.  

**8.** The method of claim 6, wherein the defined workflow comprises a further agent object configured to invoke an external model service.  

**9.** The method of claim 7, further comprising generating, by a result generator of the AI platform, a runtime signal comprising a prediction result.  

**10.** The method of claim 9, further comprising transmitting, by a signal transmitter of the AI platform, the runtime signal to the client device for rendering on a user interface of the AI platform.  

**11.** The method of claim 1, wherein the manifest file comprises authentication credentials and permission scopes associated with the machine learning model.  

**12.** The method of claim 1, wherein the machine learning model is selected from a set of predefined model classes registered with the AI platform, the set of predefined model classes comprising language models, vision models, structured data models, time series models, recommendation models, tabular models, rule-based models, graph-based models, audio processing models, multimodal models, and embedded hardware models.  

**13.** The method of claim 1, further comprising storing execution metrics associated with the callable endpoint using a monitoring component.  

**14.** The method of claim 1, wherein deploying the machine learning model comprises instantiating the machine learning model within a secure execution context managed by a code executor supporting sandbox isolation.  

**15.** The method of claim 1, further comprising applying a deployment policy using a rule enforcement component prior to provisioning the compute environment.  

**16.** The method of claim 1, further comprising registering, by the agent designer interface, the agent object in a model registry.  

**1
